mtdna.description: Most ancient mtDNA lineage, originating in Southern/Eastern Africa ~150,000-200,000 years ago. Found primarily in Khoisan peoples and other Southern African populations.
Ancient African lineage ~130,000-170,000 years old. Found in Central and West African populations, including Pygmy peoples and Hadza of Tanzania.
Common West/Central African lineage ~70,000-90,000 years old. Most common haplogroup in many West African populations. Also prevalent among African Americans (~20%).
Origin of the 'Out of Africa' migration ~60,000-70,000 years ago. All non-African mtDNA lineages descend from L3. Also common within East Africa.
Rare East African lineage found primarily in Ethiopia, Tanzania, and surrounding regions.
Rare lineage found in East Africa, particularly among Ethiopian populations.
Very rare lineage found primarily in Yemen and Ethiopia. Suggests ancient connection between Horn of Africa and Arabian Peninsula.
One of two lineages from L3 that left Africa ~60,000 years ago. Followed a 'southern route' along the coast. Dominant in South and East Asia.
Second Out-of-Africa lineage. Ancestor of most European and many Asian/Oceanian mtDNA haplogroups. ~60,000 years old.
Major branch of N giving rise to most European haplogroups (H, V, T, J, U, K). Also has Asian branches (B, F). ~55,000 years old.
Most common European haplogroup (~40-50% of Europeans). Expanded dramatically after the Last Glacial Maximum from Iberian refugium. Associated with Neolithic expansion.
Parent lineage of H and V. Found in the Near East, Caucasus, and Europe. ~25,000-30,000 years old.
European haplogroup (~5% of Europeans). Common among Basques and Sami. Expanded from Iberian refugium after Last Glacial Maximum.
Ancient European lineage ~50,000 years old. Dominant among European hunter-gatherers before Neolithic farming expansion. Multiple subclades across Europe and Near East.
Branch of U8. ~12,000 years old. Common among Ashkenazi Jews (~32%). Associated with Neolithic expansion in Europe. Ötzi the Iceman belonged to K1.
European haplogroup (~8% of Europeans). Associated with Neolithic farming expansion from the Near East ~10,000 years ago.
European haplogroup (~9% of Europeans). Associated with Neolithic farming expansion. Notable carriers include descendants of the 'Seven Daughters of Eve'. Linked to longevity in some studies.
Parent clade of haplogroups J and T. Originated in the Near East ~45,000 years ago.
Found in Europe (~2-5%) and South/West Asia. Associated with both Near Eastern Neolithic and later expansions.
Unusual distribution: found in Europe, Near East, Central Asia, and among Native Americans (especially Algonquian peoples). ~20,000-30,000 years old.
Found in Northern and Western Europe (~2-4%). More common among Scandinavians. Ancient lineage predating Neolithic expansion.
Common in Northeast Asia and the Americas. One of five founding Native American haplogroups (A, B, C, D, X). ~20,000-30,000 years old.
Common in East/Southeast Asia and the Americas. Second founding Native American lineage. Also found in Polynesia and Madagascar.
Found in Northeast Asia, Siberia, and the Americas. Third founding Native American lineage. Common among indigenous Siberians.
Common in East Asia and the Americas. Fourth founding Native American lineage. Very common in Japan (~35%) and among Tibetans.
Common in East and Southeast Asia. Found at ~15-25% frequency in many Southeast Asian populations. Related to haplogroup R.
Found in Northeast Asia, particularly among Japanese, Korean, and Tibetan populations. Subclade G2a is common in Japan.
Found in Central/Northern Asia and among Sami people of Northern Europe. Rare but widespread distribution from Korea to Finland.

ydna.description: Most ancient Y-DNA lineage, originating ~270,000 years ago. Found exclusively in Africa, particularly among Khoisan, Nilotic, and some Ethiopian populations. Represents the deepest split in the human Y-chromosome tree.
Ancient African lineage ~60,000-90,000 years old. Found primarily among Central African Pygmy populations and Hadza of Tanzania.
Ancestor of all non-African Y-DNA lineages plus some African lineages (DE). ~70,000 years old. The Out-of-Africa paternal lineage.
Split into D (Asia/Andaman/Tibet) and E (Africa/Middle East/Europe). ~65,000 years old. Represents one of the earliest branching events outside Africa.
Found in Japan (Ainu ~85%, Japanese ~35%), Tibet, Andaman Islands, and Southeast Asia. One of the earliest lineages to reach Asia via southern coastal route.
Most common haplogroup in Africa. E1b1a dominates West Africa and Bantu-speaking peoples. E1b1b (E-M215) is common in North/East Africa and spread to Southern Europe.
Ancestor of haplogroups C and F (and thus most non-African lineages). Represents the main Out-of-Africa Y-DNA branch. ~65,000 years old.
One of the earliest Out-of-Africa lineages. Found in Australia (C4), Mongolia/Central Asia (C2, including Genghis Khan's lineage), and the Americas (C-P39). ~50,000 years old.
Mega-haplogroup ancestor of G through T. ~50,000 years old. Nearly all Eurasian men descend from F through its many subclades.
Associated with Neolithic farming expansion in Europe. Found in Caucasus region (>50%), Turkey, Southern Europe. Ötzi the Iceman carried G2a. ~25,000-30,000 years old.
Very common in South Asia, especially among Dravidian-speaking populations and Roma (Gypsy) people. ~30,000-40,000 years old.
Major European haplogroup, split into I1 (Scandinavia, ~40% of Scandinavian men) and I2 (Balkans/Eastern Europe). ~25,000-30,000 years old. Dominant among pre-Neolithic European hunter-gatherers.
Parent of haplogroups I and J. ~40,000-45,000 years old. Originated in the Near East before diverging.
Dominant in the Middle East and Mediterranean. J1 common among Semitic-speaking peoples (Arabs, Jews). J2 associated with Neolithic expansion and Phoenician/Greek colonization.
Mega-haplogroup ancestor of L through T. ~45,000 years old. Major diversification event in South/Central Asia.
Found in South Asia and Central Asia. ~25,000-30,000 years old. Most common in Pakistan and northern India.
Found in Melanesia and parts of Southeast/South Asia. ~35,000-40,000 years old. Very common in Papua New Guinea.
Common in Northern Eurasia, especially Finland (~60%), Baltic states, Siberia, and among Uralic-speaking peoples. Spread with Uralic language family expansion.
Parent of haplogroups N and O. ~40,000 years old. One of the major Eurasian Y-DNA lineages.
Most common Y-DNA haplogroup in East Asia (~75% of Chinese, Japanese, Korean men). O1 in Southeast Asia/Austronesian, O2 in China/Korea/Japan. ~30,000-35,000 years old.
Parent of haplogroups Q and R. ~35,000-40,000 years old. Originated in Central/South Asia.
Found in Central/Northern Asia and dominant among indigenous peoples of the Americas (~90% of Native American men). Migrated to Americas ~15,000-20,000 years ago via Beringia.

One of the most widespread Y-DNA lineages. R1a common in South Asia, Central Asia, and Eastern Europe (associated with Indo-European expansion). R1b dominant in Western Europe (~70% of Basque, Irish, British men).
Found almost exclusively in Melanesia and Papua New Guinea. ~30,000-40,000 years old. Very common among Highland Papua New Guinean populations.
Relatively rare haplogroup found in the Middle East, East Africa, Mediterranean, and South Asia. Notable carrier: Thomas Jefferson. ~25,000-30,000 years old.
